text: The correct order of first ionisation energies of Group 1 elements is:
explanation: IE decreases down Group 1 (larger atoms, easier to lose electrons): Li(520) > Na(496) > K(419) > Rb(403) > Cs(376) kJ/mol.
name: Group 1 Ionisation Energy

text: Lithium differs from other Group 1 metals due to its diagonal relationship with:
explanation: Li resembles Mg (diagonal relationship): both form nitrides, hydroxides/carbonates are less soluble, LiCl/MgCl₂ are covalent.
name: Li Diagonal Relationship

text: The reaction of Na₂O₂ with cold water gives:
name: Na₂O₂ and Water

text: Down the group in Group 2 (alkaline earth metals), solubility of sulfates:
explanation: Sulfate solubility: BeSO₄ > MgSO₄ > CaSO₄ > SrSO₄ > BaSO₄. BaSO₄ is practically insoluble (used in barium meal X-rays).
name: Solubility of Group 2 Sulfates

text: Boron is a metalloid. Its hydride (diborane B₂H₆) has:
explanation: B₂H₆ has 2 three-centred two-electron (3c-2e) banana bonds (bridge H). Electron deficient compound.
name: Diborane Structure

text: The oxoacids of phosphorus: phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) has how many P–OH groups (proton donating)?
explanation: H₃PO₄ (orthophosphoric acid): 3 P–OH groups → tribasic acid. H₃PO₃: 2 P–OH → dibasic. H₃PO₂: 1 P–OH → monobasic.
name: Basicity of H₃PO₄

text: Which allotrope of carbon has a layered structure with delocalized electrons?
explanation: Graphite: layered hexagonal structure. Each C is sp² hybridised. Delocalized π electrons make it a conductor. Layers held by van der Waals forces.
name: Graphite Structure

text: The bond angle in NH₃ is less than 109.5° because:
options: Lone pair–bond pair repulsion is greater than bond pair–bond pair repulsion
N is more electronegative than H
explanation: NH₃ bond angle = 107°. Lone pair–bond pair (lp-bp) repulsion > bp-bp repulsion, compressing the angle from ideal 109.5°.
name: NH₃ Bond Angle

explanation: XeF₂: 2 bond pairs + 3 lone pairs on Xe → sp³d hybridization → linear shape (lone pairs in equatorial positions).
name: Shape of XeF₂

text: The thermal stability of group 17 hydrides (HF, HCl, HBr, HI) follows:
explanation: Thermal stability decreases down the group (bond strength decreases): HF > HCl > HBr > HI. Reverse: HI < HBr < HCl < HF.
name: Thermal Stability of HX

explanation: Bleaching powder: Ca(OCl)Cl (mixed salt of Ca²⁺ with OCl⁻ and Cl⁻). Formula: CaOCl₂ or Ca(OCl)Cl. Made by Cl₂ + Ca(OH)₂.
name: Bleaching Powder

text: Ozone (O₃) is a better oxidising agent than O₂ because:
explanation: O₃ → O₂ + [O]. Nascent oxygen [O] is highly reactive oxidising agent. This explains O₃'s disinfecting and bleaching ability.
name: Ozone as Oxidising Agent

explanation: Fluorine (F) is the most electronegative element (Pauling scale: 4.0). It has the smallest atomic radius among Group 17.
name: Most Electronegative Element

explanation: Plaster of Paris: \( \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} \) (hemi-hydrate). Sets by absorbing water: \( \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + \frac{3}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \).
name: Plaster of Paris

text: Interhalogen compounds like ClF₃ are formed because:
options: Larger halogens can expand their octet using d orbitals
explanation: Interhalogens: formed between two different halogens. Larger halogens (Cl, Br, I) use d orbitals to accommodate more F atoms. E.g., IF₇.
name: Interhalogen Compounds
